coredata.dc:title: Study on microwave synthesis mechanism of carbon dots based on NMR characterization
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Tang, Huang
Tang, Yuran
Xiao, Man
Zhu, He
Guo, Mingming
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have applications in various fields such as biomedicine and environmental monitoring because of its chemical stability and good biocompatibility etc. At present, one of the main points of CDs research is that the formation mechanism is not clear. The traditional hydrothermal method has a long cooling time and it is difficult to stop the reaction and obtain the product on time. Microwave reactors, however, can capture images and quickly heat and cool down. To monitor the reaction procedure and mechanism requires a special procedure. In this paper, nitrogen doped CDs (FA-CDs) with great water solubility, excellent stability and low cytotoxicity were prepared by microwave method with fumaric acid and arginine. The semi-in-situ NMR characterization of FA-CDs was carried out under different reaction times to track the reaction mechanism. The saturated chain in arginine and some double bonds in fumaric acid formed carbon nuclei, and the guanidine functional group, amino acid structures in arginine and carboxyl groups in fumaric acid were modified on the surface of FA-CDs. UV–vis absorbance spectra and fluorescence decay curves indicate that the luminescence properties of FA-CDs are mainly controlled by carbon nuclei and surface functional groups.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Formation mechanism
Carbon dots；nuclear magnetic resonance
Microwave method

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence spectra of FA-CDs with concentration effect.

Excitation- Emission- Matrix (EEM) spectra (a) and Fluorescence spectra (b) in different solvents with Ex of 315 nm of FA-CDs. (c) UV-Vis absorption spectra (Blue curve); fluorescence spectrum (Green curve) at Ex of 315 nm. (d) Fluorescence decay curve (dot plot) and fitting curve (solid line) of FA-CDs.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of FA-CDs with Ex of 315 nm with different pH; (b) fluorescence spectra of FA-CDs with Ex of 315 nm in NaCl solution with different concentration; (c) Dot and line chart of FA-CDs with continuous irradiation about 2 h; (d) Particle size distribution bar chart of FA-CDs; (e) TEM; (f) High-resolution TEM image.
XPS full spectrum (a), fine spectrum of C1s (b), fine spectrum of N1s (c), fine spectrum of O1s (d) of FA-CDs.
Fumaric acid and arginine1H NMR spectra.
Fumaric acid and arginine13C NMR spectra.
Products of different reaction time1H NMR spectra and real-time image of microwave reaction.
Products of different reaction time13C NMR spectra and real-time image of microwave reaction.
2D COSY map of the product generated after 150 min reaction.
C, N, O percentage in XPS of FA-CDs.

head.ce:title: Study on microwave synthesis mechanism of carbon dots based on NMR characterization

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of chemistry and chemical engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have applications in various fields such as biomedicine and environmental monitoring because of its chemical stability and good biocompatibility etc. At present, one of the main points of CDs research is that the formation mechanism is not clear. The traditional hydrothermal method has a long cooling time and it is difficult to stop the reaction and obtain the product on time. Microwave reactors, however, can capture images and quickly heat and cool down. To monitor the reaction procedure and mechanism requires a special procedure. In this paper, nitrogen doped CDs (FA-CDs) with great water solubility, excellent stability and low cytotoxicity were prepared by microwave method with fumaric acid and arginine. The semi-in-situ NMR characterization of FA-CDs was carried out under different reaction times to track the reaction mechanism. The saturated chain in arginine and some double bonds in fumaric acid formed carbon nuclei, and the guanidine functional group, amino acid structures in arginine and carboxyl groups in fumaric acid were modified on the surface of FA-CDs. UV–vis absorbance spectra and fluorescence decay curves indicate that the luminescence properties of FA-CDs are mainly controlled by carbon nuclei and surface functional groups.

Carbon dots (CDs) has applications in various fields such as optoelectronic devices, biomedicine, environmental monitoring and chemical analysis because of its chemical stability, good solubility, high photostability and good biocompatibility. At present, one of the main points of CDs research is that the formation mechanism is not clear. Traditional hydrothermal method has a long cooling time and it is difficult to stop the reaction and obtain the product on time. But microwave reactors can capture images and quickly raise and cool down (Scheme 1). It is a special manner to monitor the reaction procedure and mechanism. So, in this paper, nitrogen doped CDs (FA-CDs) were prepared by microwave method with fumaric acid and arginine. The semi-in-situ NMR characterization of FA-CDs under different reaction time was carried out to track the reaction mechanism. The saturated chain in arginine and some double bonds in fumaric acid formed carbon nuclei, and the guanidine functional group，amino acid structures in arginine and carboxyl groups in fumaric acid were modified on the surface of FA-CDs. UV–vis absorbance spectra and fluorescence decay curve indicate the luminescence properties of FA-CDs are mainly controlled by carbon nuclei and surface functional groups. And the prepared FA-CDs had great water solubility, excellent stability and low cytotoxicity, indicating it has great potential application.

Scheme 1 Illustration of the synthesis and characterization of FA-CDs
ga1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) as the "stars" of carbon nanomaterials that are carbon particles with size less than 10 nm have excellent photoluminescence properties and wide application prospects in photoelectric devices, biomedicine, environmental monitoring, chemical analysis, and other fields due to their advantages of good water solubility, chemical inertia, easy functionalization, high photostability, low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility [1–6]. CDs are mainly composed of carbon nuclei and functional groups on the surface or edge. The carbon nuclei are mainly sourced from dehydration carbonization after further reaction of precursor, while the surface functional groups are mainly derived from chain functional groups of precursors or solvent [3,7].

Although the research of CDs is developing rapidly, scholars focus on the synthesis of CDs owing various characteristics, and there are relatively few studies on the synthesis mechanism. Different results may be obtained by slightly changing reaction conditions due to the structural variability of CDs. Therefore, to understand the fluorescence performance of CDs, we focus on its basic architecture to explore how synthesis affects the structure and properties. Because CDs lack systematic structural features and detailed structural characterization, the exact formation mechanism and structure of CDs is in a fuzzy state at present [8]. To reveal the structure and morphology of CDs, some spectroscopy electron microscopes are the main analytic tools at present. Furthermore, few researchers also use nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectrometer to study the structural of CDs. For example, Francesca et al. successfully prepared NCNDs in water medium with arginine and ethylenediamine as precursors. It is successfully demonstrated by 13C NMR that the aromatic nucleus, aliphatic group and carbon-based region of the product mainly came from arginine, and ethylenediamine mainly contributed to the aliphatic carbon atom which was linked to heteroatom, which are demonstrated by further two-dimensional experiments [9]. Wang et al. used 13C NMR spectrum and 1H NMR spectrum to distinguish the surface functional groups of CDs. The resonance peak at 100–150 ppm of 13C NMR spectrum was attributed to sp [2] hybrid carbon atoms, while the 1H NMR spectrum shows active hydrogen signals of amino and aromatic hydrogen, further confirming that the surface of CDs embody electron-donating amino groups [10]. Liu et al. used 13C NMR spectrum and other characterization methods to certify the chemical composition of CPD. 13C NMR spectrum showed that two regions of CPD signal (13–36 ppm, 99–150 ppm) belonged to sp3 hybrid carbon and sp2 hybrid carbon, respectively [11]. Similarly, Dai’s group used NMR technology to analyze the hybrid state of carbon and compared the relative strength of conjugated sp2 and non-conjugated sp3 structures in different CDs [12]. However, most work reported so far are direct analyses of the final product by NMR spectrum, and the more detailed analyses only compare the NMR spectrum of the raw material with the product. Therefore, the collecting of NMR spectrum tracking the whole reaction process is a challenge and of great significance.

To explore the reaction process, we need to monitor changes in the system at different reaction times. The traditional hydrothermal method has a long cooling time and it is difficult to stop the reaction and obtain the product on time. Therefore, we chose a microwave reactor that can capture images and quickly heat and cool down, which is a special method for the research to monitor the reaction procedure and mechanism. Based on the above background, this work realizes tracking of the whole reaction process by collecting the 13C NMR spectrum, 1H NMR spectrum and 2D COSY spectrum of the products at different reaction times. FA-CDs are prepared by using the microwave method with water as solvent, fumaric acid with double bond structure and arginine as precursors. Microwave method in microwave reaction pro-motes dehydration and carbonization of precursors, and finally FA-CDs containing rich nitrogen and oxygen groups, such as OH, NH2 and CO groups, were formed. At the same time, combined with FTIR, UV-Vis spectrum, XPS and other characterizations were also used to analyze the structure of FA-CDs. Based on the novel methods and results, this paper proposes a formation mechanism of FA-CDs, that is, the saturated chain in arginine and part of the double bonds in fumaric acid act as carbon nuclei, and the guanidines, amino acid structures in arginine and carboxyl groups in fumaric acid act as surface functional groups to modify the surface of CDs, and finally form CDs.

L-Arginine (C6H14N4O2, Arg), Fumaric acid (C4H4O4, FA), Sodium hydroxide (NaOH), Hydrochloric acid (HCl), Sodium chloride (NaCl) (Shanghai Macklin Bioreagent Co.) Deuterium oxide (Tenglong Weibo Technology Ltd. Calf serum (Gibco, article number: 10270-106)), DMEM high glucose medium (Corning, article number: 10-013-CVRC), PBS (Gibco, article number: C10010500BT), pancreatin (Biosharp, article number: 143188), double Anti-(Hyclone, article number SV30010), DMSO (Tianjin Fuyu Fine Chemical Co., Ltd.), MTT (Solarbio). CO2 constant temperature incubator (Append China Co., Ltd., model: Galaxy 170 S), Ultra-clean workbench (Shanghai Dute Scientific Instrument Co., Ltd., model: SW-CJ-2FD), Enzyme label instrument (TECAN, model: SPARK 10 M).
All experiments used self-made ultrapure water (18.25 MΩ·cm−1) by distilled water machine.

50 mg FA and 10 mg Arg were weighed and put in a 30 mL microwave reaction flask, and 10 mL ultra-pure water was transferred into the flask, and the reaction was conducted at 220 ℃ for different reaction times (1 min,3 min,5 min,15 min,30 min,60 min,90 min,120 min and 150 min) at a stirring speed of 600 rpm by microwave synthesis reactor (Monowave 400, Anton Paar, Austria). The product with a reaction time of 120 min is FA-CDs. The initial liquid changes from white turbidized liquid to yellow brown transparent solution in the process of time. It is taken out after the reaction is cooled to 55 ℃, and then condensed by rotary evaporator (RE-52AA, Yarong Biochemical, China) at 70 °C. Then, yellow solid obtained by freeze drying is sealed by sealing film and stored in dryer for later use.

Cell culture: FA-CDs was prepared with cell medium (according to DMEM high glucose medium: calf serum: penicillin-streptomycin solution =100:10:1 ratio) to 2 mg/mL mother liquor. And then mother liquor was diluted into 500, 200, 100, 50, 20 μg/mL solution for later use. The Hela cells in the logarithmic growth phase was taken for cell count. After adjusting the cell concentration, 5 × 103/well was inoculated into a 96-well plate, and cultured in an incubator (5% CO2, 37 °C) for 24 h. After the culture is completed, the cell status is normal. 200 μL/well of complete medium and a sample solution of the specified concentration (3 multiple wells for each concentration) were added into each well, and culturing for 24 h in incubator (5% CO2, 37 °C). After that, the medium was discarded.

MTT detection: After each well washed with PBS three times, 180 μL of fresh medium and 20 μL of MTT solution (5 mg/mL) was added to each well and continue cultured for 4.0 h. Then, the original solution was discarded. 150 μL DMSO was added. After the solution shaken for 10 min, the absorbance of which at 570 nm was measured.

The relative activity was calculated by the absorbance value of each group (relative activity %= (EXPERIMENTAL group OD value - background OD value)/(control group MEAN OD value - background OD value) × 100), wherein the background OD value was the absorbance of the porous plate itself (without adding culture medium and cells).

Each sample with different reaction time was spun off most of the water at around 70 °C and then lyophilized to a solid powder. The 1H NMR and 13C NMR spectra of the products dissolved in deuterated water were collected by using 500 MHz Superconducting NMR Spectrometer (Varian INOVA AS500). 2D COSY spectra of the product generated after 150 min reaction was obtained by 80 MHz miniature nuclear magnetic resonance spectrometer (Spinsolve 80 Carbon, Magritek).

The morphology and lattice spacing of FA-CDs were observed by a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM 2100F, JEOL, Janpan). The surface functional groups of FA-CDs were characterized by attenuated total reflection Fourier transform infrared spectra (ATR-FTIR, Spectrometer Frontier, Platinum Elmer, American), and the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS，Thermo escalab 250Xi, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) spectrum of FA-CDs was collected. The obtained FA-CDs powder was prepared into 2 mg/mL solution, and the fluorescence emission spectrum (FL) and UV-Vis spectrum were collected by Fluorescence and absorbance spectrometer (Duetta, Horiba, Japan) to study its optical properties. Fluorescence quantum yield (QY) and fluorescence lifetime (τ) were obtained by steady state transient fluorescence spectrometer (FLS1000/FS5, Edinburgh, UK).

To explore the concentration effect of FA-CDs, FA-CDs solutions were prepared into 0.1 mg/mL, 0.2 mg/mL, 0.5 mg/mL, 1 mg/mL, 2 mg/mL and 5 mg/mL, respectively, and their fluorescence spectra were obtained. According to Fig. 1, the emission wavelength (Em) of FA-CDs hardly changes at the excitation wavelength (Ex) of 290–330 nm, indicating that carbon nucleus is the main emission center within this range. As the Ex increases from 330 nm to 420 nm, the Em of FA-CDs shows a phenomenon of red shift, which was attributed to the surface functional groups of FA-CDs. Quantities of surface functional groups bring about different energy levels and emission traps on the surface of CDs, which cause different Ems at different Exs [13–15]. At the same time, with the increase of solution concentration, the Em redshift and luminescence intensity show an increasing trend. The small fluctuation in the local area may be due to the self-absorption phenomenon [16]. When the concentration of FA-CDs is lower than 2 mg/mL, the best emission is found at about 385 nm under Ex of 320 nm.

Fluorescence spectra of FA-CDs with Ex of 315 nm was collected in different solvents to study the solvation effect. As shown in Fig. 2(a) and 2(b), FA-CDs has two emission regions, ultraviolet and blue, respectively, and has blue shift and red shift phenomena of varying degrees in solvents. The Em of blue and ultraviolet regions are red shifted and the intensity increases with the polarity of the solvent decreasing. These results indicate that the luminescence of blue light and ultraviolet light has different sources [17,18].

The UV-Vis absorbance spectrum (Fig. 2c) displays that FA-CDs has an absorption peak of about 285 nm, which is attributed to the π-π* transition, corresponding to the emission of its carbon nucleus in the ultraviolet region, which is consistent with the characterization result of fluorescence emission spectrum. The broadband absorption at about 350 nm is attributed to the n-π* transition. Fumaric acid and arginine bring functional groups with different surface state energy levels to FA-CDs, resulting in a variety of different emission traps on the surface, resulting in different Ems of FA-CDs at different Exs. The above results also confirm that FA-CDs has two luminescent centers, namely surface functional groups and carbon nuclei [7,17].

To further check the inference, a fluorescence decay curve of FA-CDs solution was acquired at the Ex of 315 nm, which in Fig. 2d shows double exponential attenuation. The fluorescence lifetime data in Table S1 also shows that FA-CDs have two luminescent centers, which is in accordance with the above results of UV-Vis absorbance spectrum with a lifetime (τ) of 4.2460 ns and 12.2037 ns, accounting for 69.66% and 30.34%, respectively, an average lifetime of 9.5706 ns and an absolute fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of 3% at Ex of 315 nm excitation. Based on the above results, we speculate that the carbon nucleus is the main luminescence center, and rich functional groups on the surface are the other luminescence center [17,19].

To explore the stability of FA-CDs, it was placed in solutions with different pH and NaCl concentrations prepared by hydrochloric acid and sodium hydroxide, and its fluorescence emission spectra under excitation of 315 nm were measured. The fluorescence intensity was almost unchanged when the pH changed from 2 to 13, indicating that FA-CDs has good stability in different pH environments ( Fig. 3a). However, when pH is 1, it has strong fluorescence emission in the solution, which may be caused by protonation of functional groups of FA-CDs in a strongly acidic environment. As indicated in Fig. 3(b), fluorescence intensity hardly changed in 0.5–2.5 mol/L NaCl solution, indicating the good stability of FA-CDs in NaCl solution. When light irradiated FA-CDs aqueous solution (2 mg/mL) for 2 h, its fluorescence intensity tended to be stable, indicating good optical stability (Fig. 3c) [13].

Spheroid and uniform dispersion of FA-CDs were observed clearly in TEM images. The average particle size was about 1.7 ± 0.2 nm (Figs. 3d,3e). HR-TEM images (inset of Fig. 3f) show a good lattice, about 0.21 nm, which was attributed to (100) plane of graphite, indicating that FA-CDs has a good graphite type carbon core structure [20,21]. ( Table 1).

The XPS full spectrum ( Fig. 4a) shows that FA-CDs contains Nitrogen element (399.82 eV, N1s), Carbon element (285.06 eV, C1s) and Oxygen element (531.12 eV, O1s), indicating that N atoms are successfully doped into FA-CDs. In details, the fine spectrum of C1s (Fig. 4b) reveals three peaks of about 284.88 eV (CC/C-C), 286.15 eV(C-O-C) and 287.97 eV (CO/ -CONH-), respectively. The fine spectrum of N1s (Fig. 4c) correspond to amide nitrogen (399.55 eV) and pyrrole nitrogen (401.16 eV), respectively. And the fine spectrum of O1s (Fig. 4d) correspond to C-O bond (531.07 eV) and CO bond (532.61 eV), respectively. The characterization results of XPS shows that abundant nitrogen and oxygen-containing functional groups was modified on the surface of FA-CDs [9,17,22–25].

The FTIR spectrum of FA-CDs was shown in Fig. 5, it is obvious that O-H/N-H at 3193 cm−1, C-H at 1420 cm−1, C-N at 1348 cm−1 and N-H at 1177 cm−1 came from arginine. The peaks at 3083 cm−1 (CC-H), 2541 cm−1 and 2667 cm−1 (O-H), 1659 cm−1 (CO) and 1272 cm−1 (C-OH) came from fumaric acid. The peak located at 1095 cm−1 corresponds to C-O-C, which is consistent with the result of XPS. The peak at 1575 cm−1 belongs to CC-N, and the wide peak between 2500 cm−1 and 3500 cm−1 may be due to the association of hydroxyl groups in FA-CDs. Based on the above analysis, FA-CDs contain rich surface functional groups, e.g., hydroxyl, carbonyl groups [2,3,26,27].

The 1H and 13C NMR spectra and the resonance assignments of L-arginine and fumaric acid are shown in
Figs. 6 and 7, respectively. The 1H NMR spectrum and 13C NMR spectrum of the products generated by L-arginine and fumaric acid at different reaction times were collected to explore the formation mechanism of FA-CDs, (
Figs. 8, 9).

1H NMR (Fig. 8) shows the resonant peak δ1 of CC-H in fumaric acid (6.8 ppm), δH= 2.85 ppm, 4.5 ppm formant peak are the new peak, and gradually increases with the increase of reaction time. 2D COSY spectrum ( Fig. 10) shows that the two peaks are correlated with each other, and it is speculated that the two peaks are the peaks on the -CH-CH-OH structure formed after the double bond of fumaric acid is opened. At the same time,13C NMR spectrum (Fig. 9) shows two new resonance peaks at δC= 70.2, 41.8 ppm generated, corresponding to the newly generated C on -CH-CH-OH, which verifies the inference that CC in fumaric acid opens to generate saturated chain structure.

1H NMR spectrum of Arg (Fig. 8) showed that the peaks of δ4 (3.8 ppm), δ1 (3.2 ppm), δ3(1.9 ppm) and δ2 (1.7 ppm) in arginine decreased as the reaction time increased. The resonance peak of δH (6.3 ppm) gradually increased, which was a new peak, suggesting that the saturated carbon chain in arginine was carbonized and formed unsaturated double bonds under high temperature and high pressure.

13C NMR spectra of Arg (Fig. 9) show that the resonance peak of guanidine (δa=159.7 ppm) in arginine disappeared, and the pH value increased before and after the reaction system, suggesting that the guanidine structure in arginine fell off in the form of ammonia. In addition, the disappearance of the formant peak of δe (58.5 ppm) and δf (185.9 ppm) in arginine was speculated to be due to the rupture of C(d) and C(e) of arginine, namely the shedding of α -amino acid structure. As the reaction proceeded, δC= 43.5, 30.5, 26.6 ppm resonance peak gradually weakened and eventually disappeared; it is speculated that the carbonization of saturated carbon chain as the main carbonization structure formed unsaturated double bond. The increasing of the unsaturated carbon resonance peak at δC= 136.6 ppm confirms this hypothesis, and the results were consistent with 1H NMR.

As the reaction time increased, multiple resonance peaks appeared between δC= 170–185 ppm, indicating that a variety of carbonyl functional groups were formed in the reaction process, especially in the reaction period of 15 min and 30 min, where more carbonyl resonance peaks appeared, indicating that this stage is the intermediate period of carbon point formation. As the reaction time increased, the reaction gradually completed and eventually formed stable amide, carboxylic acid, ester carbonyl functional groups. The results are consistent with the characterization analysis of IR and XPS.

Based on the above characterization results, speculating with the literature that during the generation of FA-CDs, arginine guanidine and α -amino acid structures first fall off and break, and the remaining saturated carbon chain structures cross-link with the growth of reaction time and form carbon nuclei. The double bond of fumaric acid was opened at high temperature and pressure to turn into the -CH-CH-OH structure, modifying to the surface of CDs together with the structure of arginine abscission. With the increase of reaction time, FA-CDs with abundant surface functional groups was gradually formed.

To investigate the cytotoxicity of FA-CDs, the changes of cell viability were detected when Hela cells were co-cultured with FA-CDs for 24 h by MTT assay. As shown in Fig. 11, cell viability decreased slightly with the increase of FA-CDs concentration. Moreover, 500 μg/mL FA-CDs was co-cultured with Hela cells for 24 h, and the cell survival rate was above 95% as usual, demonstrating that FA-CDs has the characteristics of low cytotoxicity and has a good prospect of biological application.

In this paper, FA-CDs was synthesized by microwave using fumaric acid and L-arginine as precursor. A series of characterization results of morphology and performances indicate that FA-CDs is spherical with an average diameter of 1.7 nm, excellent water solubility, excellent fluorescence performance and fluorescence stability, and has two emission centers. Furthermore, FA-CDs has low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility. By using this good microwave research method, we overcome the shortcomings of hydrothermal method and put forward a formation mechanism. According to NMR characterization results and literature, it is speculated that during the generation of FA-CDs, arginine guanidine group and α -amino acid structure first fall off and break, and the remaining saturated carbon chain structure crosslinks carbonization with the growth of reaction time to form carbon nuclei. The double bond of fumaric acid was opened during microwave reaction to form the -CH-CH-OH structure, modifying to the surface of CDs together with the structure of arginine abscission. As the reaction time increased, FA-CDs with abundant surface functional groups was gradually formed.

Huang Tang: Funding acquisition, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing; Yuran Tang: Conceptualization, Validation, Formal analysis, Supervision; Man Xiao: Methodology; He Zhu: Methodology; Mingming Guo: Validation, Resources, Funding acquisition, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declared that we have no conflicts of interest to this work.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We appreciate the support of the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities: Southwest University (nos. 5330500110, 5240101332), National Training Program of Innovation and Entrepreneurship for Undergraduates (no. 202110635101).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2022.129564.
